015.title: Chest Pain
015.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
015.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
015.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: 12-Lead ECG
interventions.why: STEMI vs NSTEMI vs other — do not delay for labs.

interventions.label: Aspirin 324mg
interventions.why: Chewed unless allergy. Reduces mortality in ACS.

interventions.label: Troponin + BMP
interventions.why: Baseline and trend. Risk-stratify with ECG.

interventions.label: Heparin / Anticoag
interventions.why: If ACS confirmed per protocol after ECG.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Screen for alternative causes like pneumothorax, pneumonia, widened mediastinum.

interventions.label: Nitroglycerin if indicated
interventions.why: For ongoing ischemic chest pain if no hypotension/right-ventricular infarct concern.

interventions.label: Cath lab / Admit
interventions.why: STEMI → activate PCI. Unstable → monitored bed.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 16:00 Emergency Department A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. 020.title: Palpitations
exam: Palpitations — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
020.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
020.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: 12-Lead ECG
interventions.why: STEMI vs NSTEMI vs other — do not delay for labs.

interventions.label: Aspirin 324mg
interventions.why: Chewed unless allergy. Reduces mortality in ACS.

interventions.label: Troponin + BMP
interventions.why: Baseline and trend. Risk-stratify with ECG.

interventions.label: Heparin / Anticoag
interventions.why: If ACS confirmed per protocol after ECG.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Screen for alternative causes like pneumothorax, pneumonia, widened mediastinum.

interventions.label: Nitroglycerin if indicated
interventions.why: For ongoing ischemic chest pain if no hypotension/right-ventricular infarct concern.

interventions.label: Cath lab / Admit
interventions.why: STEMI → activate PCI. Unstable → monitored bed.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
easy.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
021.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.

interventions.label: IV Fluids + NPO

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis

022.title: Found Unconscious
exam: Unresponsive on arrival, airway and circulation addressed first
Deferred until stable unless trauma pathway
GCS documented; pupils and focal signs assessed
Trauma survey for lacerations, track marks, temperature
022.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Point-of-care Glucose
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia is immediately reversible.

interventions.label: Thiamine IV
interventions.why: Before dextrose in malnourished / alcoholic patients.

interventions.label: CT Head non-contrast
interventions.why: Rule out bleed before tPA or if trauma concern.

interventions.label: Focused Neurologic Exam
interventions.why: Rapid neuro exam helps localize deficits and guides immediate imaging urgency.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps identify aspiration or pulmonary source when infection is suspected.

interventions.label: Toxicology Screen
interventions.why: Supports overdose/metabolic differential when presentation is unclear.

interventions.label: BMP · CBC · UA · Tox
interventions.why: Infection, uremia, electrolytes, overdose.

interventions.label: Monitor / ICU admit
interventions.why: Protect airway if GCS falling; continuous neuro checks.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
023.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
023.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Oxygen / BIPAP
interventions.why: Target SpO₂ per protocol; prepare intubation if tiring.

interventions.label: CXR + ECG
interventions.why: PE, pneumothorax, CHF, pneumonia patterns.

interventions.label: BNP · D-dimer · ABG
interventions.why: PE workup, CHF, CO₂ retention in COPD.

interventions.label: CT Pulmonary Angiography
interventions.why: If PE suspected and pre-test probability warrants.

interventions.label: ICU if unstable
interventions.why: BiPAP failure, shock, or rising work of breathing.

024.title: Burning During Urination
024.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 89 beat/min, Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
024.clinical_tip: Complicated UTI in men, pregnancy, or fever → admit and image.
024.objective: Treat infection; rule out pyelonephritis and STI.

interventions.label: Urinalysis + Culture
interventions.why: Culture guides therapy; send before antibiotics if stable.

interventions.label: Empiric Antibiotics
interventions.why: Cover E. coli; adjust for local resistance.

interventions.label: IV Fluids if febrile
interventions.why: Pyelonephritis and sepsis need volume.

interventions.label: CT if complicated
interventions.why: Abscess, obstruction, emphysematous pyelonephritis.

interventions.label: Urology / Admit
interventions.why: Male with fever, obstruction, or sepsis.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 9:00 Office A 19-year-old male presents to your office with complaints of pain with urination for one week.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Burning during urination History of present Illness: The patient, a 19 year old college freshman, presents to your office stating that he has a 5/10, sharp pain when he urinates. The pain stops when urination is complete. The pain began 3 days ago and has been progressively getting worse. He also recently developed testicular pain and pain with sex, especially with ejaculation. He is sexually active with his girlfriend of 6 months, and although they typically use condoms, they occasionally have unprotected sex. He also confirms chills, lethargy, a subjective fever, decreased appetite, but denies nausea and vomiting. Tylenol has improved his pain and fever. Past Medical History: Fractured ankle. Current medications: Tylenol 500mg as needed Allergies: None Vaccinations: He received his childhood vaccinations. Family History: The patient's father has hypertension. His mother has hypothyroidism. Social History: The patient is a college freshman, and had his first sexual encounter at age 15. He states that he never had a sexually transmitted infection before. He is a social drinker and has experimented with marijuana in the past. He denies any other illicit drug use. He enjoys rock climbing. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: Negative

028.title: Pelvic Pain
028.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 22 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 140 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 92 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: complaining of pelvic pain for the last month.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
028.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
028.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Pregnancy test (UPT)
interventions.why: Mandatory in reproductive-age patients.

interventions.label: Pelvic Ultrasound
interventions.why: Ectopic, ovarian torsion, abscess.

interventions.label: Abdominal Exam
interventions.why: Targeted abdominal exam helps localize pain and peritoneal signs.

interventions.label: Pelvic Exam
interventions.why: Speculum/bimanual exam helps detect tenderness, discharge, or cervical motion pain.

interventions.label: STD Screening
interventions.why: NAAT and related labs help identify infectious causes of pelvic pain.

interventions.label: IV Access + Type & Screen
interventions.why: Two large-bore if unstable or rupture concern.

interventions.label: OB/GYN Consult
interventions.why: Surgical emergency if ruptured ectopic.

interventions.label: OR / ICU if unstable
interventions.why: Unstable ectopic → surgery, not methotrexate.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 9:00 Office A 20-year-old female presents to your office with pelvic pain which has been occurring for the last month.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Pelvic pain History of present Illness: This is a 20-year-old previously healthy female presenting to your office complaining of pelvic pain for the last month. She rates the pain as a constant 7/10 sharp pain which worsens when she moves. She was encouraged by her boyfriend to come to you because she is having extreme pain during sex. Ibuprofen "takes the edge off." The pain is severe enough that she reports having difficulty sleeping. Her pain worsens during her menstruation and that the pain is "unbearable." She admits that she has had unprotected sex with her boyfriend a few times, but reports that she is taking an oral contraceptive. She denies multiple sexual partners in the past. She has had a few episodes of vomiting and still feels nauseous. No fevers or chills. She also reports pain when having a bowel movement. She denies any vaginal discharge. Past Medical History: Current medications: Oral Contraceptive Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She has never been to a gynecologist. Family History: The patient's mother has a history of breast cancer in her 50s. Her father had a 'heart attack' in his 40s. Marital/Family: The patient is the oldest of 3 children. Both of her siblings are healthy without medical problems. Personal habits: She admits to smoking 2 cigarettes a day to help lose weight. She also admits to smoking marijuana occasionally at parties. She also drinks alcohol once a week with friends. Occupation/educational: She is a college student and currently studying Art History and would like to be a teacher. Recreational: She enjoys to paint and train dogs. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: See HPI Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: Negative --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_3_Pelvic_Pain_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_3_Pelvic_Pain_chart_Vital_Signs.png

interventions.label: IV Access + Type & Screen

interventions.label: OB/GYN Consult

interventions.label: OR / ICU if unstable

032.title: Abdominal Pain

exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
032.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.

interventions.label: IV Fluids + NPO

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Surgery Consult
interventions.why: Perforation, appendicitis, mesenteric ischemia.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 18:00 Emergency Department A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room complaining of abdominal pain
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. Gastrointestinal: See HPI Genitourinary: See HPI Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_4_Abdominal_Pain_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_4_Abdominal_Pain_chart_Vital_Signs.png

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 11:00 Emergency Department A 70 year old Caucasian man comes in by EMS to the emergency room due to altered mental status.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_2_Altered_Mental_Status_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_2_Altered_Mental_Status_chart_Vital_Signs.png

standard.intro: Day 1 @ 11:00 Emergency Department A 70 year old Caucasian man comes in by EMS to the emergency room due to altered mental status.
standard.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. I has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. I used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. I now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, I got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. I was found on the floor the next morning by family members. I was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. I is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. I has no sibl

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 9:00 Office A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a 6-day history of occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 035.title: Seizure
exam: Seizure — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Neurologic exam focused on mental status and focal deficits.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
035.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Point-of-care Glucose
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia is immediately reversible.

interventions.label: Thiamine IV
interventions.why: Before dextrose in malnourished / alcoholic patients.

interventions.label: CT Head non-contrast
interventions.why: Rule out bleed before tPA or if trauma concern.

interventions.label: Focused Neurologic Exam
interventions.why: Rapid neuro exam helps localize deficits and guides immediate imaging urgency.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps identify aspiration or pulmonary source when infection is suspected.

interventions.label: Toxicology Screen
interventions.why: Supports overdose/metabolic differential when presentation is unclear.

interventions.label: BMP · CBC · UA · Tox
interventions.why: Infection, uremia, electrolytes, overdose.

interventions.label: Monitor / ICU admit
interventions.why: Protect airway if GCS falling; continuous neuro checks.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.
easy.objective: Stabilize ABCs. Identify reversible causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

I denies any history of migraines. I will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. I has never had a headache that has lasted this long. I denies any trauma to her head. I denies any sick contacts. I denies any recent antibiotic use. I is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. 040.title: Chest Pain
040.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
040.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
040.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: 12-Lead ECG
interventions.why: STEMI vs NSTEMI vs other — do not delay for labs.

interventions.label: Aspirin 324mg
interventions.why: Chewed unless allergy. Reduces mortality in ACS.

interventions.label: Troponin + BMP
interventions.why: Baseline and trend. Risk-stratify with ECG.

interventions.label: Heparin / Anticoag
interventions.why: If ACS confirmed per protocol after ECG.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Screen for alternative causes like pneumothorax, pneumonia, widened mediastinum.

interventions.label: Nitroglycerin if indicated
interventions.why: For ongoing ischemic chest pain if no hypotension/right-ventricular infarct concern.

interventions.label: Cath lab / Admit
interventions.why: STEMI → activate PCI. Unstable → monitored bed.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 16:00 Emergency Department A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 16:00 Emergency Department A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

049.title: Headache
049.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
049.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
049.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: CT Head
interventions.why: Non-contrast first for thunderclap or neuro deficit.

interventions.label: LP if CT negative
interventions.why: Xanthochromia / cell count for SAH.

interventions.label: BP Management
interventions.why: Treat only if hypertensive emergency criteria met.

interventions.label: MRI / MRA if indicated
interventions.why: Venous sinus thrombosis, posterior fossa lesions.

interventions.label: Observation / Neuro
interventions.why: Worst headache of life → admit until SAH excluded.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 9:00 Office A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a 6-day history of occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: See HPI HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: See HPI Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Vital_Signs.png

standard.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 9:00 Office A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a 6-day history of occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking.
standard.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. 051.title: Altered Mental Status
051.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
051.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Point-of-care Glucose
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia is immediately reversible.

interventions.label: Thiamine IV
interventions.why: Before dextrose in malnourished / alcoholic patients.

interventions.label: CT Head non-contrast
interventions.why: Rule out bleed before tPA or if trauma concern.

interventions.label: Focused Neurologic Exam
interventions.why: Rapid neuro exam helps localize deficits and guides immediate imaging urgency.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps identify aspiration or pulmonary source when infection is suspected.

interventions.label: Toxicology Screen
interventions.why: Supports overdose/metabolic differential when presentation is unclear.

interventions.label: BMP · CBC · UA · Tox
interventions.why: Infection, uremia, electrolytes, overdose.

interventions.label: Monitor / ICU admit
interventions.why: Protect airway if GCS falling; continuous neuro checks.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 11:00 Emergency Department A 70 year old Caucasian man comes in by EMS to the emergency room due to altered mental status.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. 052.title: Chest Pain
052.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
052.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
052.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: 12-Lead ECG
interventions.why: STEMI vs NSTEMI vs other — do not delay for labs.

interventions.label: Aspirin 324mg
interventions.why: Chewed unless allergy. Reduces mortality in ACS.

interventions.label: Troponin + BMP
interventions.why: Baseline and trend. Risk-stratify with ECG.

interventions.label: Heparin / Anticoag
interventions.why: If ACS confirmed per protocol after ECG.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Screen for alternative causes like pneumothorax, pneumonia, widened mediastinum.

interventions.label: Nitroglycerin if indicated
interventions.why: For ongoing ischemic chest pain if no hypotension/right-ventricular infarct concern.

interventions.label: Cath lab / Admit
interventions.why: STEMI → activate PCI. Unstable → monitored bed.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 16:00 Emergency Department A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

easy.intro: Day 1 @ 16:00 Emergency Department A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: I, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, I developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. I rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied I to the hospital says that this has never happened to I in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.
easy.vitalsText: The team is checking my vital signs now.
easy.clinicalTip: You feel unwell and want help — the clinical team will guide next steps.
easy.objective: Share your symptoms clearly; ask what happens next.

053.title: Shortness of Breath

053.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Oxygen / BIPAP
interventions.why: Target SpO₂ per protocol; prepare intubation if tiring.

interventions.label: CXR + ECG
interventions.why: PE, pneumothorax, CHF, pneumonia patterns.

interventions.label: BNP · D-dimer · ABG
interventions.why: PE workup, CHF, CO₂ retention in COPD.

interventions.label: CT Pulmonary Angiography
interventions.why: If PE suspected and pre-test probability warrants.

interventions.label: ICU if unstable
interventions.why: BiPAP failure, shock, or rising work of breathing.

054.title: Yellow Baby
exam: Yellow Baby — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Warm skin with fever; no purpura on initial survey.
054.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
054.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Empiric Antibiotics IV
interventions.why: Do not delay for LP if unstable or petechial rash.

interventions.label: IV Fluid Bolus
interventions.why: Septic shock physiology common in toxic presentations.

interventions.label: Blood Cultures
interventions.why: Before antibiotics when possible — not if delaying care.

interventions.label: ICU / Isolation
interventions.why: Droplet precautions for meningococcal concern.

interventions.label: Dermatology / ID consult
interventions.why: Toxic epidermal necrolysis, meningococcemia, vasculitis.

056.title: Abdominal Pain
056.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
056.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
056.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: IV Fluids + NPO
interventions.why: Resuscitate before imaging if hypotensive.

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Surgery Consult
interventions.why: Perforation, appendicitis, mesenteric ischemia.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 18:00 Emergency Department A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room complaining of abdominal pain
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. 058.title: Shortness of Breath
exam: Dyspneic, speaking in short phrases if severe
058.clinical_tip: ABG or pulse ox + ECG + CXR in the first minutes.
058.objective: Stabilize hypoxia. Differentiate pulmonary vs cardiac vs metabolic.

interventions.label: Oxygen / BIPAP
interventions.why: Target SpO₂ per protocol; prepare intubation if tiring.

interventions.label: CXR + ECG
interventions.why: PE, pneumothorax, CHF, pneumonia patterns.

interventions.label: BNP · D-dimer · ABG
interventions.why: PE workup, CHF, CO₂ retention in COPD.

interventions.label: CT Pulmonary Angiography
interventions.why: If PE suspected and pre-test probability warrants.

interventions.label: ICU if unstable
interventions.why: BiPAP failure, shock, or rising work of breathing.

060.title: Abdominal Pain
060.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 18:00 Temperature: 37.2 C (98.9 degrees F) Pulse: 106 beat/min, Tachycardic Respiratory rate: 20 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 114 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 79 mm Hg Height: 160.0 cm (63.0 in) Weight: 80.5 kg (177.4 lb) Body Mass Index: 31.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
060.clinical_tip: Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal exam findings until rupture.
060.objective: Resuscitate, image, involve surgery early if indicated.

interventions.label: IV Fluids + NPO
interventions.why: Resuscitate before imaging if hypotensive.

interventions.label: CBC · Lipase · LFTs · UA
interventions.why: Narrow differential: biliary, pancreatic, UTI, medical.

interventions.label: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
interventions.why: High yield for appendicitis, diverticulitis, obstruction.

interventions.label: Surgery Consult
interventions.why: Perforation, appendicitis, mesenteric ischemia.

interventions.label: Antibiotics if indicated
interventions.why: Give before OR if sepsis or perforation suspected.

061.title: Headache
061.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 38.3 C (101.0 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 118 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 70 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days.
Abdominal tenderness pattern matches history; no rigid abdomen documented yet.
Mental status intact; cranial nerves and neck stiffness assessed.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
061.clinical_tip: Thunderclap headache = SAH workup. Do not send home without considering red flags.
061.objective: Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass, and hypertensive emergency.

interventions.label: CT Head
interventions.why: Non-contrast first for thunderclap or neuro deficit.

interventions.label: LP if CT negative
interventions.why: Xanthochromia / cell count for SAH.

interventions.label: BP Management
interventions.why: Treat only if hypertensive emergency criteria met.

interventions.label: MRI / MRA if indicated
interventions.why: Venous sinus thrombosis, posterior fossa lesions.

interventions.label: Observation / Neuro
interventions.why: Worst headache of life → admit until SAH excluded.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 9:00 Office A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a 6-day history of occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Headache History of present Illness: A 55-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension presents to your office with a an occipital headache, mild vertigo, and a tendency to fall when walking for 6 days. She states that she never had any problems walking or being off-balance. Eight days ago, she stated that she had a pink rash on her arms and torso and felt feverish. The rash improved after a couple days, but the headache and tendency to fall persisted. She had nausea, but she denied any vomiting. She feels weak and "under the weather." She denies travel, but went hiking 2 weeks ago nearby her house which she routinely does every summer. She denies any history of migraines. She will occasionally get a tension headache which she will treat with Ibuprofen. She has never had a headache that has lasted this long. She denies any trauma to her head. She denies any sick contacts. She denies any recent antibiotic use. She is very concerned about her symptoms. PMH: Past Medical History: Hypertension. The patient recently had a colonoscopy screening which was normal. Current medications: HCTZ 25mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: Her father has a history of Hypertension and died of a stroke. Her mother has no health problems and is still living. Social History: Marital/Family: She is married with one child. She lives in Gaithersburg, Maryland. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She lives an active lifestyle. She drinks alcohol socially. She denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She works as a public relations specialist. She is a college graduate with a BS in communication. Recreational: The patient enjoys to bike and hike. She loves the outdoors and spends as much time as she is able. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: See HPI HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: See HPI Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_5_Headache_chart_Vital_Signs.png

Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
062.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Point-of-care Glucose
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia is immediately reversible.

interventions.label: Thiamine IV
interventions.why: Before dextrose in malnourished / alcoholic patients.

interventions.label: CT Head non-contrast
interventions.why: Rule out bleed before tPA or if trauma concern.

interventions.label: Focused Neurologic Exam
interventions.why: Rapid neuro exam helps localize deficits and guides immediate imaging urgency.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps identify aspiration or pulmonary source when infection is suspected.

interventions.label: Toxicology Screen
interventions.why: Supports overdose/metabolic differential when presentation is unclear.

interventions.label: BMP · CBC · UA · Tox
interventions.why: Infection, uremia, electrolytes, overdose.

interventions.label: Monitor / ICU admit
interventions.why: Protect airway if GCS falling; continuous neuro checks.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 11:00 Emergency Department A 70 year old Caucasian man comes in by EMS to the emergency room due to altered mental status.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_2_Altered_Mental_Status_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_2_Altered_Mental_Status_chart_Vital_Signs.png

exam: presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 18:00 Emergency Department A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room complaining of abdominal pain
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 16:00 Emergency Department A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

standard.intro: Day 1 @ 16:00 Emergency Department A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest
standard.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Chest pain; respiratory distress History of present Illness: I, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance’s arrival, I developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. I rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied I to the hospital says that this has never happened to I in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.
standard.vitalsText: What I was told about my vitals is on the monitor.

076.title: Fever and Rash
exam: Fever and Rash — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
076.clinical_tip: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
076.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection.

interventions.label: Empiric Antibiotics IV
interventions.why: Do not delay for LP if unstable or petechial rash.

interventions.label: IV Fluid Bolus
interventions.why: Septic shock physiology common in toxic presentations.

interventions.label: Blood Cultures
interventions.why: Before antibiotics when possible — not if delaying care.

interventions.label: ICU / Isolation
interventions.why: Droplet precautions for meningococcal concern.

interventions.label: Dermatology / ID consult
interventions.why: Toxic epidermal necrolysis, meningococcemia, vasculitis.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Petechiae + fever in a sick patient — treat empirically for meningococcemia.
easy.objective: Recognize toxic rash syndromes. Resuscitate and cover infection. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

077.title: Chest Pain
077.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 16:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 120 beats/min, Weak Respiratory rate: 34 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 100 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 60 mm Hg Height: 183cm (72.0 in) Weight: 97.5 kg (215 lb) Body Mass Index: 29.1 kg/m2
exam: Chest Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
077.clinical_tip: Until proven otherwise, treat unstable chest pain as ACS.
077.objective: Rule out life threats. ECG within 10 minutes.

interventions.label: 12-Lead ECG
interventions.why: STEMI vs NSTEMI vs other — do not delay for labs.

interventions.label: Aspirin 324mg
interventions.why: Chewed unless allergy. Reduces mortality in ACS.

interventions.label: Troponin + BMP
interventions.why: Baseline and trend. Risk-stratify with ECG.

interventions.label: Heparin / Anticoag
interventions.why: If ACS confirmed per protocol after ECG.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Screen for alternative causes like pneumothorax, pneumonia, widened mediastinum.

interventions.label: Nitroglycerin if indicated
interventions.why: For ongoing ischemic chest pain if no hypotension/right-ventricular infarct concern.

interventions.label: Cath lab / Admit
interventions.why: STEMI → activate PCI. Unstable → monitored bed.

078.title: Generalized Weakness
078.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 9:00 Temperature: 37.0 C (98.6 degrees F) Pulse: 110 beat/min, Regular rhythm; bounding Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 122 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 74 mm Hg Height: 175.3 cm (69.0 in) Weight: 65.8 kg (145 lb) Body Mass Index: 21.4 kg/m2
exam: presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Motor strength and reflexes tested for fatigability and focal deficits.
Skin rash morphology and distribution documented.
078.clinical_tip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
078.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

interventions.label: Glucose · BMP
interventions.why: Hypo/hyperkalemia, hypercalcemia, hypoglycemia.

interventions.label: ECG + Monitor
interventions.why: HyperK causes weakness and arrhythmia.

interventions.label: MRI Spine if cord signs
interventions.why: Cord compression is a surgical emergency.

interventions.label: Tox screen if indicated
interventions.why: Botulism, organophosphates, sedatives.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 9:00 Office A 30-year-old female presents to your office with complaints of generalized weakness for weeks
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Generalized weakness History of present Illness: A previously healthy 30-year-old female with a PMH of asthma presents to your office complaining of progressive generalized weakness that has been occurring for the past 3 months. Her symptoms worsen at the end of the day, and her weakness improves with rest. She reports occasional blurry vision with improvement after closing and resting her eyes. She also notes difficulty chewing food and swallowing recently. She has also been experiencing dyspnea with strenuous exercise, but it is now refractory to her albuterol inhaler. Past Medical History: She broke her arm when she was 14 which required an open reduction and pin placement. Current medications: Birth control, multi-vitamin Allergies: Penicillin (rash and shortness of breath) Vaccinations: Received Childhood vaccinations. Women's health: She contracted gonorrhea from a sexual partner at age 20 which was successfully treated. She had a LEEP procedure done due to an abnormal pap smear 6 years ago. Her last pap smear was done 3 years ago which was normal. Family History: Her father has a history of congestive heart failure and is status post two myocardial infarctions. Her mother has hypothyroidism. Her grandfather died of a stroke. Social History: Marital/Family: The patient is single. She is currently living and is sexually active with her boyfriend of 2 years. Personal habits: She states that she drinks occasionally. The patient smokes cigars with her boyfriend on occasion when going out. Occupation/educational: She works as a newspaper editor for a local newspaper. Recreational: She enjoys reading and breeds dogs. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: See HPI Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 18:00 Emergency Department A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room complaining of abdominal pain
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Abdominal Pain History of present Illness: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency room with abdominal pain, lethargy, and vomiting x2 days. Her vomiting is non-bloody. Her abdominal pain is diffuse, which she rates as a 6/10. She also claims to have had an increased appetite prior to having these symptoms. She also claims that she’s been developing blurry vision, dizziness, and drowsiness. She also states that she is having increased urination Past Medical History Past Medical History: None Current medications: Birth control Allergies: None Vaccinations: The patient received her childhood vaccinations. She has never received any HPV vaccines. Women's health: The patient cannot remember when her last pap smear was done. Family History: The patient has a brother with Diabetes. Social History: Marital/Family: Single. She is an undergraduate student at a local university. Personal habits: The patient denies smoking. She drinks socially. She smoked marijuana "a few times" with her friends. She denies other illicit drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: She is a student at a local university Recreational: She plays the piano and sings in a band. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Her skin is dry. HEENT: Her throat is dry. Cardiorespiratory: She feels that her heart is beating faster. 085.title: Amenorrhea
exam: Amenorrhea — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
085.clinical_tip: Positive pregnancy test changes everything — ectopic until proven otherwise.
085.objective: Rule out ectopic, torsion, and rupture.

interventions.label: Pregnancy test (UPT)
interventions.why: Mandatory in reproductive-age patients.

interventions.label: Pelvic Ultrasound
interventions.why: Ectopic, ovarian torsion, abscess.

interventions.label: Abdominal Exam
interventions.why: Targeted abdominal exam helps localize pain and peritoneal signs.

interventions.label: Pelvic Exam
interventions.why: Speculum/bimanual exam helps detect tenderness, discharge, or cervical motion pain.

interventions.label: STD Screening
interventions.why: NAAT and related labs help identify infectious causes of pelvic pain.

interventions.label: IV Access + Type & Screen
interventions.why: Two large-bore if unstable or rupture concern.

interventions.label: OB/GYN Consult
interventions.why: Surgical emergency if ruptured ectopic.

interventions.label: OR / ICU if unstable
interventions.why: Unstable ectopic → surgery, not methotrexate.

093.title: Altered Mental Status
093.vitalsText: Initial Vital Signs Day 1 @ 11:00 Temperature: 36.8 C (98.2 degrees F) Pulse: 88 beats/min. Regular rhythm Respiratory rate: 18 /minute Blood pressure, systolic: 115 mm Hg Blood pressure, diastolic: 85 mm Hg Height: 180cm (70.9 in) Weight: 91.0 kg (200.6 lb) Body Mass Index: 27.9 kg/m2
exam: Decreased engagement and cognitive change compared with reported baseline.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Altered mental status with attention and command-following documented.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
093.clinical_tip: Fingerstick glucose before CT — hypoglycemia mimics stroke.

interventions.label: Point-of-care Glucose
interventions.why: Hypoglycemia is immediately reversible.

interventions.label: Thiamine IV
interventions.why: Before dextrose in malnourished / alcoholic patients.

interventions.label: CT Head non-contrast
interventions.why: Rule out bleed before tPA or if trauma concern.

interventions.label: Focused Neurologic Exam
interventions.why: Rapid neuro exam helps localize deficits and guides immediate imaging urgency.

interventions.label: Chest X-ray
interventions.why: Helps identify aspiration or pulmonary source when infection is suspected.

interventions.label: Toxicology Screen
interventions.why: Supports overdose/metabolic differential when presentation is unclear.

interventions.label: BMP · CBC · UA · Tox
interventions.why: Infection, uremia, electrolytes, overdose.

interventions.label: Monitor / ICU admit
interventions.why: Protect airway if GCS falling; continuous neuro checks.

easy.intro: Case Introduction Day 1 @ 11:00 Emergency Department A 70 year old Caucasian man comes in by EMS to the emergency room due to altered mental status.
easy.hpi: Initial History Reason(s) for visit: Altered mental status History of present Illness: A 70-year-old Caucasian man comes to the Emergency room because his family members are concerned that something is wrong. The patient has been having behavioral changes over a period of three or four weeks. He used to be very active and managed all of his financial affairs. He now barely talks, stares at the wall for hours at a time, and sleeps most of the day. Four weeks ago, the patient got up to urinate and fell before reaching the bathroom. He was found on the floor the next morning by family members. He was taken to his family doctor and was discharged with a prescription to buy a cane. Since that time, he has been having problems walking. There have been no fevers or night sweats. The patient is currently in no pain. Past Medical History: Chronic alcohol abuse; cataracts. Hospitalization Procedures: Hemorrhoidectomy 1 year ago. Last colonoscopy was 4 years ago which demonstrated no abnormalities. Current medications: Aspirin 81mg PO daily Allergies: None Vaccinations: Up to date Family History: Father: Deceased due to a 'heart attack'. Mother: Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of natural causes. The patient has no siblings. Social History: Marital/Family: Widowed; he has three children. Personal habits: the patient smokes 1 Pack/day. He currently does not drink alcohol but was a chronic alcoholic for 30 years. Denies use of illegal drugs or misuse of medications. Occupation/educational: He is a retired Army human resources manager. He is a high school graduate. Recreational: The patient likes to spend time with his children and enjoys collecting stamps. Review of systems: General: See HPI Skin: Negative HEENT: Negative Cardiorespiratory: Negative Gastrointestinal: Negative Genitourinary: Negative Neuropsychiatric: See HPI --- Chart tabs --- * Chart: Progress Notes → presentation_2_Altered_Mental_Status_chart_Progress_Notes.png * Chart: Vital Signs → presentation_2_Altered_Mental_Status_chart_Vital_Signs.png

interventions.label: Tox screen if indicated
interventions.why: Botulism, organophosphates, sedatives.

interventions.label: Admit / Neuro consult
interventions.why: Progressive weakness or respiratory concern → ICU.

easy.clinicalTip: Teaching hint: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
easy.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes. Start with stabilization, then targeted testing.

standard.clinicalTip: Weakness + hyperreflexia vs hyporeflexia localizes — think electrolytes and cord.
standard.objective: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

hard.clinicalTip: Minimal coaching — prioritize life threats without hand-holding.
hard.objective: High-acuity workup: Differentiate metabolic, neurologic, and toxic causes.

easy.vitalsText: The team is checking my vital signs now.
easy.clinicalTip: You feel unwell and want help — the clinical team will guide next steps.
easy.objective: Share your symptoms clearly; ask what happens next.

standard.clinicalTip: Focus on what you feel, when it started, and what makes it worse or better.
standard.objective: Participate in shared decision-making as the team stabilizes you.

hard.clinicalTip: Limited guidance — advocate for yourself if symptoms worsen.
hard.objective: Navigate uncertainty while the team works through the differential.

099.title: Back Pain
exam: Back Pain — exam tailored to chief complaint on arrival.
Soft, non-distended, no focal peritoneal signs on initial exam.
Alert and oriented unless perfusion or metabolic derangement present.
No acute rash; capillary refill and perfusion assessed.
099.clinical_tip: Saddle anesthesia + bowel/bladder dysfunction = surgical emergency.
099.objective: Urgent MRI and emergent decompression consult.

interventions.label: Emergency Department
interventions.why: Acute cord compression syndrome.

interventions.label: Urgent MRI spine
interventions.why: Confirm compressive lesion level.

interventions.label: Emergency neurosurgery / ortho consult
interventions.why: Order with MRI — do not wait for full workup.

interventions.label: Bladder scan / catheter if retention
interventions.why: Document urinary retention.

interventions.label: CBC + BMP
interventions.why: Pre-op labs and baseline.

interventions.label: Neurologic checks
interventions.why: Track progressive deficits.
